text: Gamma-ray energy absorption coefficients were computed as a function of energy for 24 elements: hydrogen, carbon, beryllium, nitrogen, oxygen, magnesium, sodium, aluminum, silicon, sulfur, phosphorus, argon, potassium, iron, calcium, copper, molybdenum, iodine, tin, tungsten, platinum, lead, thallium, and uranium.
Satisfactory molten-salt hydrodynamic bearing films were established in two tests of an INOR-8 bearing and an INOR-8 journal.

A conventional aluminum bearing and an Inconel journal that had been lubricated with Dowtherm "A" (a eutectic mixture of diphenyl and diphenyloxide) during 3200 hr of operation in a pump that was circulating NaF-ZrF4-UF4 at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ was examined.

The oil-lubricated centrifugal-pump rotary element assembly being operated in a gamma-irradiation facility at the MTR had accumulated a total of 5312 hr of operation and a gamma-ray dose rate to the lower seal region of $9.3 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{r}$ by the end of the quarter.
Materials required for a motor suitable for long-term operation at $1250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in a radiation field are being investigated.
During continuous isothermal operation of this pump for 2500 hr at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , with a molten salt as the pumped fluid, there has been no leakage of lead from the seal.
A similar lead-sealed pump with a $3\frac{1}{4}$ -in.-dia shaft is being fabricated for testing.
Two large freeze-flange joints were tested in a 4-in. line carrying a high-temperature molten salt.
There was no salt leakage, but gas leakage of the flanges was slightly in excess of the allowable $10^{-7}$ cm³ of helium per second.
Heater-and-insulation units designed for use on a 4-in. line were tested along with the large freeze flange.
The thermal loss from two units was found to be about four times the heat loss from an equivalent length of "Hy-Temp" pipe insulation 3 in. thick.
Exposure of Inconel and INOR-8 specimens to aluminum chloride vapor for 1000 hr showed that either material would be satisfactory for construction of the thermal-convection loop.

The enthalpies, heat capacities, and heats of fusion were determined for the mixtures $\mathsf{NaF - BeF}_2$ - $\mathsf{UF}_4$ (53-46-1 mole %), $\mathsf{LiF - BeF}_2$ - $\mathsf{UF}_4$ (53-46-1 mole %), $\mathsf{NaCl - CaCl}_2$ (49-51 mole %), $\mathsf{LiCl - KCl}$ (70-30 mole %), $\mathsf{LiCl - KCl}$ (60-40 mole %), and $\mathsf{LiCl - KCl}$ (50-50 mole %).
Preliminary values were obtained for the surface tension of $\mathsf{LiF - BeF}_2$ - $\mathsf{UF}_4$ (62-37-1 mole %) over the temperature range of 460 to $750^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ .

The cause of a major discrepancy in heat balance in the study of the heat-transfer coefficients of LiF-BeF $_2$ UF $_4$ (53-46-1 mole %) flowing in a small-diameter Inconel tube is being investigated.

The results of a series of tests of Inconel resistance-type fuel-level-indicating elements have not been reproducible, as yet, but it has been established that fuel 130 has sufficient resistance to provide a useful milliwatt output from the probe.

Graphite-moderated molten-salt reactors fueled with low-enrichment material were studied, and the results of preliminary calculations indicated that initial enrichments as low as $1.25\% \mathrm{U}^{235}$ might be used in a circulating-fuel system.
Highly enriched uranium would be added as makeup fuel, and such reactors could probably be operated to burnups as high as 60,000 Mwd/ton.
A simplified natural-convection molten-salt reactor for operation at 576 Mw (thermal) was studied to determine the approximate size of components and the fuel volume.
Two Inconel and four INOR-8 thermal-convection loops, which circulated various fused-fluoride-salt mixtures, were examined.
An Inconel loop which operated 1000 hr at $1350^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ with the mixture LiF- $\mathsf{BeF}_2\mathsf{-UF}_4$ (62-37-1 mole %) was attacked to a depth of 3 mils.
A loop operated previously with this mixture at $1250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ showed approximately 1 mil of attack over a similar test period.
Operation of an Inconel loop at $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for 4360 hr with the mixture NaF-LiF-KF (11.5-46.5-42 mole %) resulted in the formation of subsurface voids to a depth of 2 mils.
No attack was evident in any of the INOR-8 loops even after operation, in one case, for 3114 hr.

A loop which circulated the mixture NaF-ZrF4-UF4 (57-42-1 mole %) and also contained a secondary sodium circuit showed maximum attack to a depth of 1.5 mils in the salt circuit and only slight surface roughening in the sodium circuits.
Mixtures of LiF, BeF2, and UF4 produced attacks of 5 and 8 mils respectively in two other
loops operated for slightly more than 3000 hr at a maximum hot-leg temperature of $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
INOR-8 samples removed from a forced-circulation loop that was shut down after 3370 hr of operation for repair of a leak at a heater lug were found to have no evidence of surface attack.
The mixture $\mathsf{LiF - BeF}_2\cdot \mathsf{UF}_4$ (53-46-1 mole %) is being circulated in this loop.
The maximum wall temperature is $1300^{\circ}\mathsf{F}$ .
A sodium-graphite system was used to prepare Inconel and INOR-8 tensile test specimens for a study of the effect of carburization on the mechanical properties.
The carburization at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ caused increases in both the yield and tensile strengths of Inconel and decreased the ductility at room temperature.
For the INOR-8 specimens, the yield strength was increased, the tensile strength was slightly decreased, and the ductility was greatly reduced.
In similar tests at $1250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ the carburized specimens again showed a trend to lower ductility, but some specimens were more ductile than control specimens.

In comparative tests of the carburization of INOR-8 by fuel 130-graphite systems, 3-mil surface cuts of specimens exposed only to fuel analyzed 640 ppm carbon, while similar cuts of specimens exposed to fuel containing a graphite rod analyzed 940 ppm carbon.
For a test of the penetration of graphite by fuel 130, a graphite crucible $4\frac{1}{2}$ in. long with a tapered hole 0.43 in.
ID and $3\frac{1}{2}$ in. deep was loaded with the fuel and tested in a vacuum at $1300^{\circ}F$ .
Radiographs were made at 500-hr intervals to study the penetration of the graphite by the fuel.

At the end of the first 500 hr test of a CCN graphite crucible, there had been no penetration of the graphite, but a disk of $\mathrm{UO}_2$ had formed at the bottom of the tapered hole that was 0.1 in. thick.
showed that it contained $28\%$ of the uranium originally present in the fuel.
Further tests with TSF in contact with fuel 130 have shown similar precipitation of $\mathrm{UO}_2$ but to a lesser extent.
Further it is in contrast to the lack of precipitation in similar tests in which fuel 30 (NaF-ZrF4-UF4, 50-46-4 mole %) was used.
A time-temperature-stress relationship was formulated for the creep strength of INOR-8 to aid in predicting long-time creep strengths.
Heat-to-heat variation studies indicated a $0.14\%$ -carbon-content heat to be appreciably stronger than two other INOR-8 heats containing 0.02 and $0.06\%$ carbon.
Specimens of commercially fabricated INOR-8 were aged for 5000 hr at test temperatures of from 1000 to $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Room- and elevated-temperature mechanical property tests of INOR-8 weld metal both in the as-welded and in the welded and aged conditions were made and the results were compared with similar data for Hastelloy B and Hastelloy W.
Aging seriously reduces the room-temperature ductility of Hastelloy B and Hastelloy W weld metal, while the ductility of INOR-8 weld metal is improved.
The influence of melting practice and carbide spheroid-ization on the elevated-temperature ductility of INOR-8 is being studied.

Two fluoride-fuel-filled graphite capsules were examined that had been irradiated in the MTR for 1610 and 1492 hr at $1250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Studies have revealed the cause of low concentration of uranium in 50-lb batches of $\mathsf{LiF - BeF}_2$ . $\mathsf{UF}_4$ (62-37-1 mole %) that were transferred from 250-lb batches.
The first liquid to form when the mixture is melted is the eutectic composition containing 3.1 wt % uranium rather than the nominal 6.5 wt % uranium in the 250-lb batch.

Solubility studies were made on several systems, including argon in $\mathsf{LiF - BeF}_2$ (64-36 mole $\%$ ); HF in LiF-BeF mixtures containing 10 to 50 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{BeF}_2;$ CeF in NaF-LiF-BeF solvents of various compositions; $\mathsf{CeF}_3,$ LaF, and SmF in LiF-BeF $^2$ UF4 (62.8-36.4-0.8 mole $\%$ ; CeF and LaF simultaneously in LiF-BeF-UF4 (62.8-36.4-0.8 mole $\%$ ) and CeF and SmF simultaneously in LiF-BeF $^2$ UF4 (62.8-36.4-0.8 mole $\%$ ).

The values were calculated from measurements of the electromotive force of cells containing pure nickel and nickel-molybdenum alloy electrodes in a bath of NaCl-KCl eutectic containing 5 wt $\%$ NiCl $_2$ .
Trial runs gave a value of 195 dynes/cm at 425 to $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for LiF-BeF $_2$ -UF $_4$ (53-46-1 mole %) and 196 dynes/cm at about $480^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for LiF-BeF $_2$ (63-37 mole %).

Similar tests in solvents containing $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ , which is considered to be more basic than $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ , showed that in an LiF- $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ mixture containing 52 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ the disproportionation of $\mathsf{CrF}_2$ was roughly comparable to that in a solvent containing 35 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ .
Further measurements of the activity coefficients of $\mathsf{CrF}_2$ dissolved in the molten mixture NaF-ZrF4 (53-47 mole %) were determined and equilibrium quotients were calculated.

The results of the measurements of $\mathsf{CrF}_2$ and the corresponding investigations of NiF2 and FeF2, along with the values for the activities of chromium in alloys, can now be used in the study of corrosion of Inconel and INOR-8 alloys.

The results indicated that the amount of attack of Inconel can be expected to be proportional to the pressure of the aluminum chloride vapor and that at 5 atm the corrosion after 300 hr would be about 0.7 mil.
INOR-8, in which the activity of chromium is much lower than in Inconel, should prove quite resistant to aluminum chloride vapor.

The NaF-LiF-KF mixture has a lower density and a lower viscosity than the LiF-MgF $_{2}$ mixture.

The drain system required for a 600-Mw (thermal) molten-salt reactor system was considered, and it was found that four vessels 5 ft in diameter and 12 ft high would be needed to contain the 600 ft³ of salt from the reactor.
Each of the vessels would be capable of removing 1.8 Mw of after heat within the practical temperature limitations of the container materials.

On the basis that each plant had the same net electrical output, the maximum deviation in the power cost contributed by the capital charge was found to be approximately 1 mill/kwhr.
A 30-Mw experimental reactor is being considered that would have a 6-ft-dia core enclosed in a $\frac{1}{2}$ -in.-thick INOR-8 pressure vessel.
Surrounding the core there would be a 6-in.-thick layer of insulation, a 6-in.-thick air space, and five 2-in.-thick boron steel thermal shields cooled by 4-in.-thick layers of diphenyl.

Nuclear calculations indicate that the critical mass at $1180^{\circ}F$ would be $49\mathrm{kg}$ of $\mathsf{U}^{235}$ , and the total inventory for a system volume of 150 ft would be $65\mathrm{kg}$ of $\mathsf{U}^{235}$ .
After a year of operation, the critical mass would have increased to $61\mathrm{kg}$ with a net integrated burnup of $14.4\mathrm{kg}$ .
Approximately $7\%$ of the neutrons would be captured in the core vessel.
This compares with $5\%$ for the 600-Mw reference design reactor.

Hydrodynamic Bearings. - The results of the first attempt to establish a molten salt $(\mathsf{LiF - BeF}_2\mathsf{-UF}_4)$ 62-37-1 mole $\%$ ) hydrodynamic bearing film have been assessed and three additional tests with the same combination of materials, an INOR-8 bearing and an INOR-8 journal, have been made.
Testing of a second bearing and journal of INOR-8 was terminated on schedule at the end of a 500-hr period of satisfactory operation.
Fifty-seven stops and starts were made during a 54-hr period of the test.
The remainder of the test was conducted at constant conditions, that is, a shaft speed of 1200 rpm and an applied radial load of 200 lb.
A report<sup>2</sup> covering the first and second tests was written.
The third test covered a period of 284 hr during which 37 stop-start tests were performed.
The last 100 hr of operation were at constant conditions: shaft speed, 1200 rpm; applied radial load, 300 lb.

A fourth test with a new (third) bearing and journal of INOR-8 in molten salt at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ is under way to trace the cause of rubbing marks to either stop and start tests or to steady-load operation.
Hydrostatic Bearings. - Fabrication of the hydrostatic bearing tester<sup>3</sup> was completed and tests were run on two hydrostatic bearings with water as the lubricant.
During the first test, in which a radial clearance of 0.003 in. was used, the bearing and journal experienced considerable wear.
The partitions were removed for the second test, which was conducted with a second hydrostatic bearing having a radial clearance of 0.0075 in.
Fabrication of a mount for testing awaits a satisfactory solution to the problem of brazing molybdenum to Inconel and to INOR-8.

Organic-Liquid-Lubricated Bearings. - The high-temperature sump pump being used to conduct a demonstration of the behavior of Dowtherm "A" (eutectic mixture of diphenyl and diphenyloxide) as a bearing lubricant operated satisfactorily for a period of 3206 hr.

The pump circulated fuel 30 $(\text{NaF}-\text{ZrF}_4 \cdot \text{UF}_4, 50-46-4 \text{ mole}\%)$ at a temperature of $1200^{\circ}\text{F}$ ; the shaft speed was about 2600 rpm; the temperature of the Dowtherm "A" supplied to the bearings was maintained at $180^{\circ}\text{F}$ , with approximately a $5^{\circ}\text{F}$ rise through the test bearings.
The lubricant flow rate was 2 gpm.
Upon completion of 3200 hr of operation, the pump was disassembled, and the test bearings and seals were inspected.
contact ball bearings (MRC-5309), which were submerged in the lubricant during operation, showed no detectable wear or imperfections.
The diametral clearance between the aluminum bearing and the Inconel journal was found to be essentially the same (0.0044 in.) as it was at the time of the previous 100-hr inspection.
Seal nose heights were measured at the beginning and the end of a 2008-hr test period, and wear indications of 0.0016 in. on the upper seal and 0.0004 in. on the lower seal were noted.
Lower seal leakage averaged $2\mathrm{cm}^3/$ day during the operating period, but the upper seal leakage was essentially zero.
After 6 hr of operation, that is, an over-all total of 3206 hr of operation, a building power interruption caused the lube oil pump to stop.
Within about 30 sec, it was noticed that the drive equipment was heavily overloaded and that the pump speed had been reduced to zero.
It was further observed that the three oil-supply grooves in the bearing were not covered by the journal for approximately $\frac{1}{4}$ in.

Bearing and Seal Gamma Irradiation. - The centrifugal-pump rotary element assembly being operated without an impeller in a gamma-irradiation facility at the MTR had accumulated a total of 5312 hr of operation by the end of the quarter; during 4426 hr of the operating period, the assembly has been exposed to gamma irradiation.
The accumulated gamma-ray dose to the lower seal region has reached approximately $9.3 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{r}$ .
The bulk oil has accumulated an approximate gamma-ray dose of $2.25 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{r}$ .
On June 12, 1958, a momentary power failure stopped shaft rotation and caused seal leakage of $53\mathrm{cm}^3$ in the following 24 hr.
During the succeeding 24 hr the leakage was $12\mathrm{cm}^3$ , after which the leakage leveled off to the normal 6 to $7\mathrm{cm}^3/$ day.

Previously, aging heat treatments for times up to 2000 hr in the temperature range 1000 to $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ were shown to cause no significant changes in the tensile properties of the material.
The two heats being studied contain different amounts of carbon, however, and they have significantly different properties, as shown in Tables 2.1.10 and 2.1.11.

The effect of a 5000-hr heat treatment was determined during the quarter, and the results for specimens from the two heats are compared with similar data for annealed specimens from the two heats in Tables 2.1.10 and 2.1.11.
As may be seen, aging for 5000 hr did not significantly change the tensile properties.

It is therefore concluded on the basis of these tensile tests that INOR-8 does not exhibit instabilities that would be detrimental in service for extended times in the temperature range 1000 to $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

The compound $\mathsf{LiF}\cdot 2\mathsf{ThF}_4$ , however, can take as much as 5 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ into solid solution and $3\mathsf{LiF}\cdot 6\mathsf{ThF}_4$ can take as much as 11 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ .

Moreover, the introduction of $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ into the structure of $3\mathsf{LiF}\cdot 6\mathsf{ThF}_4$ apparently causes enough distortion of the lattice for some $7\mathsf{LiF}\cdot 6\mathsf{ThF}_4$ also to be taken into solid solution.

Thus a roughly triangular area of homogeneous solid solution exists with one apex located on the $\mathsf{LiF}\cdot 6\mathsf{ThF}_4$ boundary at the composition $3\mathsf{LiF}\cdot 6\mathsf{ThF}_4$ and the other two apices at about the compositions 67 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{LiF}$ -11 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ -22 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ThF}_4$ and 60 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{LiF}$ -13 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ -27 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ThF}_4$ .

Detailed quenching tests have established that there is practically no solid solution of $7\mathsf{LiF}\cdot 6\mathsf{ThF}_4$ in $3\mathsf{LiF}\cdot 6\mathsf{ThF}_4$ in the binary system $\mathsf{LiF}\cdot 6\mathsf{ThF}_4$ .

With the increases of $7\mathsf{LiF}\cdot 6\mathsf{ThF}_4$ in $3\mathsf{LiF}\cdot 6\mathsf{ThF}_4$ in the ternary system, the refractive indices of $3\mathsf{LiF}\cdot 6\mathsf{ThF}_4$ are raised somewhat and the birefringence is lowered.

The compound $2\mathsf{LiF}\cdot 6\mathsf{BeF}_2$ apparently exists only as the pure compound in the ternary system, and there is no evidence that it enters into solid solution with any of the $\mathsf{LiF}\cdot 6\mathsf{ThF}_4$ compounds.

Tests were also made in solvents containing $\mathsf{BeF}_{2}$ , which is considered to be more basic, or less acidic, than $\mathsf{ZrF}_{4}$ , since it is a better fluoride donor for furnishing fluorides for complexing.
An appreciable amount of disproportionation, $\mathsf{CrF}_{3} / \mathsf{CrF}_{2} = 0.15$ for 2 wt $\%$ Cr at $800^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ , was found in LiF-BeF $_2$ (48-52 mole $\%$ ).
This is roughly comparable to the amount of disproportionation in the 35 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_{4}$ solvent.

In another $\mathsf{BeF}_{2}$ mixture (LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ , 67-23-10) the effect of ThF $_4$ (acidic) was more than counterbalanced by the increased LiF (basic) content to give a net increase in the complexing of $\mathsf{CrF}_{3}$ .

Here the equilibrium $\mathsf{CrF}_{3} / \mathsf{CrF}_{2}$ ratio was about 0.4 at $800^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ with 2 wt $\%$ Cr; thus the solvent containing ThF $_4$ has about the same complexing tendency as the 15 mole $\%$ ZrF $_4$ solvent.

These results confirm the supposition that $\mathsf{BeF}_{2}$ mixtures are more basic than corresponding ZrF $_4$ mixtures and that the extent of disproportionation is greater in the more basic solvents.
No disproportionation of $\mathsf{FeF}_2$ was detected in any of the solvent compositions at either 600 or $800^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ .

Studies of chromium diffusion in chromium-containing nickel-base alloys were continued along the lines described previously.32 New equipment is being developed for the depletion-method experiments that are based on the rate of loss of $\mathbf{Cr}^{51}$ (as $\mathbf{Cr}^{+4*}$ ) from a noncorrosive salt contained in the alloy being studied.
The constant-potential method was utilized further to prepare specimens for the study of the distribution of $\mathbf{Cr}^{51}$ within the alloy.

In these investigations, measurements are made of the gain of $\mathbf{Cr}^{51}$ by a metal specimen exposed to molten salt containing a dissolved amount of chromium 51 (as $\mathbf{Cr}^{++*}$ ) that remains constant with time.

The diffusion coefficients resulting from initial experiments, which were conducted with specimens which had been polished but not hydrogen fired, were one to two orders of magnitude higher than those obtained with specimens that had been polished and then hydrogen fired at 1100 to $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 3 hr.

The fluoride volatilization process, which was developed for heterogeneous reactor fuel processing and was used successfully for recovery of the uranium from the fuel mixture $(\mathrm{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4})$ circulated in the Aircraft Reactor Experiment (ARE), appears to be applicable to processing fuels of the type now being considered for the molten-salt reactor.1 Sufficient laboratory work has been done to confirm that fluorination of the fuel salts LiF-BeF2-UF4 or LiF-BeF2-UF4-ThF4 results in good recovery of the uranium.

The salt-recovery process is based on the solubility of $\mathsf{LiF - BeF}_2$ in highly concentrated aqueous HF (70-100%) and the insolubility of rare-earth and other polyvalent-element fluorides, which results in the recovered salt being decontaminated from the important rare-earth neutron poisons.
